# Atom Best Practices | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

This guide provides comprehensive best practices for creating effective, reusable Atoms that contribute to a high-quality Intuition knowledge graph.

Creating Effective Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#creating-effective-atoms "Direct link to Creating Effective Atoms")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1\. Leverage Deterministic IDs[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#1-leverage-deterministic-ids "Direct link to 1. Leverage Deterministic IDs")

Remember that identical atomData will always produce the same Atom ID. This ensures:

*   No duplicate Atoms for the same data
*   Predictable, verifiable identifiers
*   Consistent references across the network

    // Same data = same Atom IDconst atom1 = createAtom({ data: "Ethereum" })const atom2 = createAtom({ data: "Ethereum" })// atom1.id === atom2.id  (always true)

### 2\. Check for Similar Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#2-check-for-similar-atoms "Direct link to 2. Check for Similar Atoms")

Before creating new Atoms, search for existing canonical Atoms:

*   Reduces fragmentation
*   Leverages existing Signal
*   Improves data consistency
*   Benefits from accumulated trust

### 3\. Use Clear, Descriptive Data[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#3-use-clear-descriptive-data "Direct link to 3. Use Clear, Descriptive Data")

Choose unambiguous, descriptive data values:

**Good:**

    { "data": "Machine Learning" }{ "data": "Vitalik Buterin" }{ "data": "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z" }

**Avoid:**

    { "data": "ML" }  // Ambiguous{ "data": "VB" }  // Unclear{ "data": "today" }  // Not specific

### 4\. Maintain Single Purpose[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#4-maintain-single-purpose "Direct link to 4. Maintain Single Purpose")

Each Atom should represent one thing:

**Good:**

    const ethereum = { data: "Ethereum" }const blockchain = { data: "blockchain" }const platform = { data: "platform" }// Combine via Triple[Ethereum] - [is a] - [blockchain platform]

**Avoid:**

    // Too much packed into one Atomconst composite = { data: "Ethereum is a blockchain platform founded in 2015" }

### 5\. Consider Reusability[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#5-consider-reusability "Direct link to 5. Consider Reusability")

Design Atoms that others will want to reference:

*   Use common terminology
*   Follow domain conventions
*   Keep scope focused
*   Think composably

Atom Design Patterns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#atom-design-patterns "Direct link to Atom Design Patterns")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Think of Atoms as **words in the Intuition dictionary**:

*   They are the lego-like pieces that snap into many contexts
*   Community Signal concentrates on the words that matter most
*   Triples form the "sentences" that connect these dictionary words together

### Pattern: Universal Concepts[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#pattern-universal-concepts "Direct link to Pattern: Universal Concepts")

Create Atoms for widely-applicable concepts:

    [true][false][yes][no][member of][created by][owns]

### Pattern: Domain-Specific Terms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#pattern-domain-specific-terms "Direct link to Pattern: Domain-Specific Terms")

Use precise domain language:

    [smart contract][ERC-20 token][liquidity pool][validator node]

### Pattern: Temporal Markers[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#pattern-temporal-markers "Direct link to Pattern: Temporal Markers")

Include specific timestamps or dates:

    [2024-01-15][Q1 2024][2024-01-15T10:30:00Z]

Integration with Triples[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#integration-with-triples "Direct link to Integration with Triples")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Atoms gain their true power when connected via Triples:

### Subject Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#subject-atoms "Direct link to Subject Atoms")

The entity being described:

    [Alice][Ethereum][USDC Token]

### Predicate Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#predicate-atoms "Direct link to Predicate Atoms")

The relationship or property:

    [owns][created][member of][deployed on]

### Object Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#object-atoms "Direct link to Object Atoms")

The value or target:

    [Bob][blockchain][Ethereum network][2024-01-15]

This separation allows each component to be independently verified, updated, and trusted.

Naming Conventions[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#naming-conventions "Direct link to Naming Conventions")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Consistency Rules[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#consistency-rules "Direct link to Consistency Rules")

1.  **Use camelCase for property names** in structured data
2.  **Maintain consistent terminology** across related atoms
3.  **Follow established domain conventions**
4.  **Use singular form** unless specifically plural

### Examples[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#examples "Direct link to Examples")

**Good:**

    {  "type": "concept",  "content": "Smart Contract",  "category": "blockchain"}

**Consistent:**

    {  "type": "entity",  "content": "Uniswap Protocol",  "category": "defi"}

Metadata Best Practices[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#metadata-best-practices "Direct link to Metadata Best Practices")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Required Metadata[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#required-metadata "Direct link to Required Metadata")

Always include:

*   Creation timestamps
*   Creator's DID
*   Version information for mutable atoms

    {  "metadata": {    "created": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",    "creator": "did:ethr:mainnet:0x123...",    "version": "1.0",    "lastModified": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"  }}

### Optional but Recommended[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#optional-but-recommended "Direct link to Optional but Recommended")

    {  "metadata": {    "description": "Brief description of the Atom",    "tags": ["relevant", "keywords"],    "category": "domain-category",    "source": "https://source-url.com",    "license": "CC-BY-4.0"  }}

Scalable Design[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#scalable-design "Direct link to Scalable Design")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Design for Composability[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#design-for-composability "Direct link to Design for Composability")

Create Atoms that can be combined in multiple ways:

    // Reusable building blocksconst alice = { data: "Alice" }const bob = { data: "Bob" }const friendOf = { data: "friend of" }const knows = { data: "knows" }// Multiple compositions[Alice] - [friend of] - [Bob][Alice] - [knows] - [Bob]

### Consider Future Extensibility[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#consider-future-extensibility "Direct link to Consider Future Extensibility")

Design with growth in mind:

*   Use versioning
*   Include extension points
*   Maintain backward compatibility
*   Document changes

### Maintain Backward Compatibility[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#maintain-backward-compatibility "Direct link to Maintain Backward Compatibility")

When updating Atoms:

*   Preserve existing fields
*   Add new fields carefully
*   Document breaking changes
*   Consider migration paths

Common Pitfalls to Avoid[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#common-pitfalls-to-avoid "Direct link to Common Pitfalls to Avoid")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1\. Overly Broad Atoms[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#1-overly-broad-atoms "Direct link to 1. Overly Broad Atoms")

**Avoid:**

    { "data": "Technology" }  // Too broad

**Better:**

    { "data": "Blockchain Technology" }{ "data": "AI Technology" }

### 2\. Redundant Information[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#2-redundant-information "Direct link to 2. Redundant Information")

**Avoid:**

    {  "data": "Ethereum blockchain cryptocurrency"}

**Better:**

    // Separate Atoms connected via Triples[Ethereum] - [is a] - [blockchain][Ethereum] - [is a] - [cryptocurrency]

### 3\. Time-Sensitive Data Without Timestamps[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#3-time-sensitive-data-without-timestamps "Direct link to 3. Time-Sensitive Data Without Timestamps")

**Avoid:**

    { "data": "Current CEO" }

**Better:**

    [Company X] - [has CEO] - [Person Y]// Add temporal context via Triple metadata

### 4\. Ambiguous References[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#4-ambiguous-references "Direct link to 4. Ambiguous References")

**Avoid:**

    { "data": "Paris" }  // City or person?

**Better:**

    { "data": "Paris, France" }{ "data": "Paris Hilton" }

Performance Considerations[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#performance-considerations "Direct link to Performance Considerations")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Optimize for Queries[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#optimize-for-queries "Direct link to Optimize for Queries")

*   Use indexed fields
*   Keep data structures flat when possible
*   Minimize nested complexity
*   Consider query patterns

### Balance Detail and Size[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#balance-detail-and-size "Direct link to Balance Detail and Size")

*   Include necessary context
*   Avoid bloat
*   Use references for large data
*   Store heavy content off-chain (IPFS)

Quality Checklist[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#quality-checklist "Direct link to Quality Checklist")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Before creating an Atom, verify:

*   [ ]  Clear, unambiguous data
*   [ ]  Single, focused purpose
*   [ ]  Follows naming conventions
*   [ ]  Includes required metadata
*   [ ]  Checked for existing similar Atoms
*   [ ]  Designed for reusability
*   [ ]  Appropriate granularity
*   [ ]  Verifiable content
*   [ ]  Respects community standards
*   [ ]  Documented if complex

* * *

Next Steps[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-concepts/primitives/Atoms/best-practices#next-steps "Direct link to Next Steps")

# Activity Feed | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/activity-feed#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Build an activity feed showing recent protocol activity.

Query[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/activity-feed#query "Direct link to Query")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GetRecentActivity($limit: Int!) {
  deposits(
    order\_by: { created\_at: desc }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    id
    sender\_id
    assets\_after\_fees
    created\_at
    vault {
      term {
        atom { label }
      }
    }
  }
  redemptions(
    order\_by: { created\_at: desc }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    id
    receiver\_id
    assets
    created\_at
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

*   [Query](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/activity-feed#query)

---

# Client Setup | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The Intuition GraphQL API works with any GraphQL client in any language. This guide provides setup examples for popular clients across different languages.

JavaScript / TypeScript[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#javascript--typescript "Direct link to JavaScript / TypeScript")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For JavaScript/TypeScript projects, import endpoints from the package instead of hardcoding them:

    import { API_URL_PROD, API_URL_DEV } from '@0xintuition/graphql'// API_URL_PROD = 'https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql'// API_URL_DEV = 'https://testnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql'

### graphql-request[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#graphql-request "Direct link to graphql-request")

Lightweight and simple GraphQL client:

    import { GraphQLClient } from 'graphql-request'import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const client = new GraphQLClient(API_URL_PROD)const query = `  query GetAtom($id: String!) {    atom(term_id: $id) {      term_id      label      image    }  }`const data = await client.request(query, { id: '0x...' })console.log(data.atom)

**Installation**:

    npm install graphql-request graphql

[graphql-request documentation](https://github.com/jasonkuhrt/graphql-request)

### Apollo Client[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#apollo-client "Direct link to Apollo Client")

Full-featured GraphQL client with caching and React integration:

    import { ApolloClient, InMemoryCache, gql } from '@apollo/client'import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const client = new ApolloClient({  uri: API_URL_PROD,  cache: new InMemoryCache()})const { data } = await client.query({  query: gql`    query GetAtom($id: String!) {      atom(term_id: $id) {        term_id        label        image      }    }  `,  variables: { id: '0x...' }})console.log(data.atom)

**Installation**:

    npm install @apollo/client graphql

[@apollo/client documentation](https://www.apollographql.com/docs/react/)

### urql[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#urql "Direct link to urql")

Highly customizable and lightweight GraphQL client:

    import { createClient } from 'urql'import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const client = createClient({  url: API_URL_PROD})const query = `  query GetAtom($id: String!) {    atom(term_id: $id) {      term_id      label    }  }`const result = await client.query(query, { id: '0x...' }).toPromise()console.log(result.data.atom)

**Installation**:

    npm install urql graphql

[urql documentation](https://formidable.com/open-source/urql/docs/)

### Plain fetch[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#plain-fetch "Direct link to Plain fetch")

No dependencies required:

    import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const response = await fetch(API_URL_PROD, {  method: 'POST',  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },  body: JSON.stringify({    query: `      query GetAtom($id: String!) {        atom(term_id: $id) {          term_id          label        }      }    `,    variables: { id: '0x...' }  })})const { data } = await response.json()console.log(data.atom)

Python[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#python "Direct link to Python")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### gql[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#gql "Direct link to gql")

Type-safe Python GraphQL client:

    from gql import gql, Clientfrom gql.transport.requests import RequestsHTTPTransporttransport = RequestsHTTPTransport(url='https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql')client = Client(transport=transport)query = gql('''  query GetAtom($id: String!) {    atom(term_id: $id) {      term_id      label      image    }  }''')result = client.execute(query, variable_values={'id': '0x...'})print(result['atom'])

**Installation**:

    pip install gql[requests]

[gql documentation](https://gql.readthedocs.io/)

### python-graphql-client[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#python-graphql-client "Direct link to python-graphql-client")

Simple and lightweight GraphQL client:

    from python_graphql_client import GraphqlClientclient = GraphqlClient(endpoint='https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql')query = '''  query GetAtom($id: String!) {    atom(term_id: $id) {      term_id      label      image    }  }'''data = client.execute(query=query, variables={'id': '0x...'})print(data['data']['atom'])

**Installation**:

    pip install python-graphql-client

[python-graphql-client documentation](https://github.com/prisma-labs/python-graphql-client)

Go[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#go "Direct link to Go")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### machinebox/graphql[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#machineboxgraphql "Direct link to machinebox/graphql")

Simple Go GraphQL client:

    package mainimport (    "context"    "fmt"    "log"    "github.com/machinebox/graphql")func main() {    client := graphql.NewClient("https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql")    req := graphql.NewRequest(`        query GetAtom($id: String!) {            atom(term_id: $id) {                term_id                label                image            }        }    `)    req.Var("id", "0x...")    var response struct {        Atom struct {            TermID string `json:"term_id"`            Label  string `json:"label"`            Image  string `json:"image"`        } `json:"atom"`    }    if err := client.Run(context.Background(), req, &response); err != nil {        log.Fatal(err)    }    fmt.Printf("Atom: %+v\n", response.Atom)}

**Installation**:

    go get github.com/machinebox/graphql

[machinebox/graphql documentation](https://github.com/machinebox/graphql)

Rust[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#rust "Direct link to Rust")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### graphql-client[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#graphql-client "Direct link to graphql-client")

Type-safe GraphQL client for Rust:

    use graphql_client::{GraphQLQuery, Response};use reqwest;#[derive(GraphQLQuery)]#[graphql(    schema_path = "schema.graphql",    query_path = "get_atom.graphql",)]struct GetAtom;#[tokio::main]async fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {    let client = reqwest::Client::new();    let variables = get_atom::Variables {        id: "0x...".to_string(),    };    let response = client        .post("https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql")        .json(&GetAtom::build_query(variables))        .send()        .await?        .json::<Response<get_atom::ResponseData>>()        .await?;    if let Some(data) = response.data {        println!("Atom: {:?}", data.atom);    }    Ok(())}

**Installation**:

    [dependencies]graphql_client = "0.13"reqwest = { version = "0.11", features = ["json"] }tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }

[graphql-client documentation](https://github.com/graphql-rust/graphql-client)

Error Handling[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#error-handling "Direct link to Error Handling")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All GraphQL responses follow the standard error format:

    {  "data": null,  "errors": [    {      "message": "Field 'nonexistent_field' not found in type 'atoms'",      "extensions": {        "path": "$.selectionSet.atoms.selectionSet.nonexistent_field",        "code": "validation-failed"      }    }  ]}

**Common error types**:

*   **validation-failed**: Query syntax or schema validation error
*   **constraint-violation**: Database constraint violated
*   **permission-denied**: Access to restricted field (not applicable for public API)
*   **unexpected**: Internal server error

**Best practices for error handling**:

    // Example with graphql-requestimport { GraphQLClient, ClientError } from 'graphql-request'try {  const data = await client.request(query, variables)  console.log('Success:', data)} catch (error) {  if (error instanceof ClientError) {    console.error('GraphQL errors:', error.response.errors)    console.error('Status:', error.response.status)  } else {    console.error('Network error:', error)  }}

Next Steps[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/getting-started/client-setup#next-steps "Direct link to Next Steps")

# Triple Events | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Functions for parsing triple-related events from transaction receipts.

eventParseTripleCreated[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#eventparsetriplecreated "Direct link to eventParseTripleCreated")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse TripleCreated events from a transaction.

### Parameters[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#parameters "Direct link to Parameters")

| Parameter | Type | Description | Required |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| publicClient | `PublicClient` | Viem public client | Yes |
| txHash | `Hash` | Transaction hash | Yes |

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#returns "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    tripleId: bigint    subjectId: bigint    predicateId: bigint    objectId: bigint    counterVaultId: bigint    creatorAtomId: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#example "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseTripleCreated } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseTripleCreated(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('Triple ID:', event.args.tripleId)  console.log('Subject:', event.args.subjectId)  console.log('Predicate:', event.args.predicateId)  console.log('Object:', event.args.objectId)  console.log('Counter vault:', event.args.counterVaultId)})

* * *

eventParseTripleDepositCreated[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#eventparsetripledepositcreated "Direct link to eventParseTripleDepositCreated")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse TripleDepositCreated events.

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#returns-1 "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    tripleId: bigint    sender: Address    receiver: Address    assets: bigint    shares: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#example-1 "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseTripleDepositCreated } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseTripleDepositCreated(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('Triple ID:', event.args.tripleId)  console.log('Deposit:', formatEther(event.args.assets))  console.log('Shares:', formatEther(event.args.shares))})

* * *

See Also[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/triple-events#see-also "Direct link to See Also")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Vault Events](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events)

# Choose Appropriate Operators | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Use the right comparison operator for better performance.

Anti-Pattern[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#anti-pattern "Direct link to Anti-Pattern")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # BAD: Using _ilike for exact matchesquery GetAccount($address: String!) {  accounts(where: { id: { _ilike: $address } }) {    id    label  }}

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # GOOD: Use _eq or primary key lookupquery GetAccount($address: String!) {  account(id: $address) {    id    label  }}

Operator Guidelines[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#operator-guidelines "Direct link to Operator Guidelines")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **\_eq**: Exact matches (fastest)
*   **\_ilike**: Case-insensitive pattern matching (slower)
*   **\_in**: Multiple values
*   **\_gt/\_lt**: Comparisons
*   **Primary key lookup**: Most efficient (when possible)

*   [Anti-Pattern](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#anti-pattern)
    
*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#best-practice)
    
*   [Operator Guidelines](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/comparison-operators#operator-guidelines)

---

# Use Database Functions | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Use backend functions for complex queries instead of client-side filtering.

Anti-Pattern[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#anti-pattern "Direct link to Anti-Pattern")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # BAD: Manual filtering for social queriesquery GetFollowingManually($address: String!) {  my_positions: positions(where: { account_id: { _eq: $address } }) {    vault {      term {        triple {          # Complex filtering in application code...        }      }    }  }}

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # GOOD: Using database functionsquery GetFollowingEfficiently($address: String!) {  following(args: { address: $address }) {    id    label    atom {      term_id      label    }  }}

Available Functions[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#available-functions "Direct link to Available Functions")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `following`: Get accounts a user follows
*   `positions_from_following`: Social feed of positions
*   `search_term`: Semantic search
*   `signals_from_following`: Activity from followed accounts

Benefits[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#benefits "Direct link to Benefits")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **Faster execution**: Runs in database
2.  **Less data transfer**: Filtered server-side
3.  **More maintainable**: Logic in one place

*   [Anti-Pattern](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#anti-pattern)
    
*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#best-practice)
    
*   [Available Functions](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#available-functions)
    
*   [Benefits](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/database-functions#benefits)

---

# Trust Bonding Events | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/trust-bonding-events#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Functions for parsing Trust Bonding events from transaction receipts.

eventParseRewardsClaimed[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/trust-bonding-events#eventparserewardsclaimed "Direct link to eventParseRewardsClaimed")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse RewardsClaimed events from a transaction.

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/trust-bonding-events#returns "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    user: Address    amount: bigint    epoch: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/trust-bonding-events#example "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseRewardsClaimed } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseRewardsClaimed(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('User:', event.args.user)  console.log('Rewards claimed:', formatEther(event.args.amount))  console.log('Epoch:', event.args.epoch)})

* * *

Generic Event Parser[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/trust-bonding-events#generic-event-parser "Direct link to Generic Event Parser")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For parsing any event from protocol contracts:

    import { eventParse, MultiVaultAbi, TrustBondingAbi } from '@0xintuition/protocol'// Parse any MultiVault eventconst events = await eventParse(  publicClient,  txHash,  MultiVaultAbi,  'EventName')// Parse any TrustBonding eventconst bondingEvents = await eventParse(  publicClient,  txHash,  TrustBondingAbi,  'EventName')

* * *

# Pagination Best Practices | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pagination#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Always use limit and offset for efficient pagination with consistent ordering.

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pagination#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    query GetAtomsPage($limit: Int!, $offset: Int!) {  total: atoms_aggregate {    aggregate {      count    }  }  atoms(    limit: $limit    offset: $offset    order_by: { created_at: desc }  ) {    term_id    label  }}

Key Points[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pagination#key-points "Direct link to Key Points")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **Always include order\_by** for consistent results
2.  **Fetch total count** using aggregates
3.  **Use reasonable limits** (10-100 items per page)
4.  **Calculate offset** as `(page - 1) * limit`

*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pagination#best-practice)
    
*   [Key Points](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pagination#key-points)

---

# Atom with Vault | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Fetch atom metadata along with vault statistics.

Use Case[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#use-case "Direct link to Use Case")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Display atom information with market data on a detail page.

Query[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#query "Direct link to Query")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GetAtomWithVault($atomId: String!, $curveId: numeric!) {
  atom(term\_id: $atomId) {
    term\_id
    label
    image
    type
    created\_at
    creator {
      id
      label
    }
    term {
      vaults(where: { curve\_id: { \_eq: $curveId } }) {
        curve\_id
        total\_shares
        total\_assets
        current\_share\_price
        market\_cap
        position\_count
      }
    }
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

Implementation[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#implementation "Direct link to Implementation")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    import { GraphQLClient } from 'graphql-request'import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const client = new GraphQLClient(API_URL_PROD)const query = `  query GetAtomWithVault($atomId: String!, $curveId: numeric!) {    atom(term_id: $atomId) {      term_id      label      image      term {        vaults(where: { curve_id: { _eq: $curveId } }) {          total_shares          market_cap          position_count        }      }    }  }`const data = await client.request(query, {  atomId: '0x...',  curveId: '1'})

*   [Use Case](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#use-case)
    
*   [Query](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#query)
    
*   [Implementation](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/atom-with-vault#implementation)

---

# Efficient Filtering | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Filter early and use indexed fields for better performance.

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    query GetFilteredAtoms($type: atom_type!, $since: timestamptz!) {  atoms(    where: {      type: { _eq: $type }      created_at: { _gte: $since }    }    limit: 100  ) {    term_id    label  }}

Indexed Fields[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#indexed-fields "Direct link to Indexed Fields")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These fields have database indexes:

*   `term_id` (primary key)
*   `creator_id`
*   `type`
*   `created_at`

Operator Selection[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#operator-selection "Direct link to Operator Selection")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Use `_eq` for exact matches (not `_ilike`)
*   Use `_ilike` only for pattern matching
*   Use `_in` for multiple values
*   Combine filters with `_and` for specificity

*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#best-practice)
    
*   [Indexed Fields](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#indexed-fields)
    
*   [Operator Selection](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/filtering#operator-selection)

---

# Subscriptions vs Polling | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Choose the right approach for your data update needs.

Use Subscriptions When[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#use-subscriptions-when "Direct link to Use Subscriptions When")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Building real-time dashboards
*   Monitoring live protocol activity
*   Data changes frequently (multiple times per minute)
*   User expects immediate updates
*   Creating notification systems

**Example:**

    subscription WatchMyPositions($cursor: [positions_stream_cursor_input]!) {  positions_stream(cursor: $cursor, batch_size: 10) {    id    shares    vault { current_share_price }  }}

Use Polling When[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#use-polling-when "Direct link to Use Polling When")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Data updates infrequently (e.g., daily statistics)
*   Real-time updates aren't critical for UX
*   Minimizing server connections is important
*   Building static reports or analytics

**Example:**

    query GetStats {  stats {    total_accounts    total_atoms    total_triples  }}# Poll every 30 seconds or on user action

Guidelines[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#guidelines "Direct link to Guidelines")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Polling interval**: 30-60 seconds for most use cases
*   **Subscription batch\_size**: 10-50 items
*   **Handle reconnections**: Store last cursor for resumability

*   [Use Subscriptions When](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#use-subscriptions-when)
    
*   [Use Polling When](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#use-polling-when)
    
*   [Guidelines](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/subscriptions-vs-polling#guidelines)

---

# Use Fragments | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Reuse field selections across queries with GraphQL fragments.

Anti-Pattern[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#anti-pattern "Direct link to Anti-Pattern")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # BAD: Duplicating field selectionsquery GetTriple($id: String!) {  triple(term_id: $id) {    subject {      term_id      label      creator { id label }    }    predicate {      term_id      label      creator { id label }    }    object {      term_id      label      creator { id label }    }  }}

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # GOOD: Using fragmentsfragment AtomBasics on atoms {  term_id  label  creator {    id    label  }}query GetTriple($id: String!) {  triple(term_id: $id) {    subject { ...AtomBasics }    predicate { ...AtomBasics }    object { ...AtomBasics }  }}

Benefits[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#benefits "Direct link to Benefits")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **DRY code**: Define once, use everywhere
2.  **Easier maintenance**: Update in one place
3.  **Consistent data**: Same fields across queries
4.  **Better readability**: Named, semantic units

*   [Anti-Pattern](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#anti-pattern)
    
*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#best-practice)
    
*   [Benefits](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/fragments#benefits)

---

# Complex Filtering | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/complex-filtering#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Implement multi-criteria search across entities.

Query[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/complex-filtering#query "Direct link to Query")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GlobalSearch($searchTerm: String!, $limit: Int!) {
  accounts(
    where: {
      \_or: \[\
        { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } }\
        { atom: { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } } }\
      \]
    }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    id
    label
    image
  }
  atoms(
    where: { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    term\_id
    label
    image
    type
  }
  triples(
    where: {
      \_or: \[\
        { subject: { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } } }\
        { predicate: { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } } }\
        { object: { label: { \_ilike: $searchTerm } } }\
      \]
    }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    term\_id
    subject { label }
    predicate { label }
    object { label }
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

*   [Query](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/complex-filtering#query)

---

# Image & IPFS Operations | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The Intuition GraphQL API provides mutations for uploading images and JSON data to IPFS (InterPlanetary File System). These operations return IPFS hashes that can be used in atom creation.

Available Operations[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#available-operations "Direct link to Available Operations")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Operation | Description |
| --- | --- |
| [`uploadImage`](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/upload-image) | Upload a base64-encoded image to IPFS |
| [`uploadImageFromUrl`](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/upload-image-from-url) | Upload an image from a URL to IPFS |
| [`uploadJsonToIpfs`](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/upload-json-to-ipfs) | Upload JSON metadata to IPFS |

Use Cases[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#use-cases "Direct link to Use Cases")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Atom Creation Workflow[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#atom-creation-workflow "Direct link to Atom Creation Workflow")

When creating atoms with images or metadata:

1.  Upload image using `uploadImage` or `uploadImageFromUrl`
2.  Upload JSON metadata using `uploadJsonToIpfs`
3.  Use returned IPFS hashes in atom creation

### Profile Images[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#profile-images "Direct link to Profile Images")

Upload profile images for accounts:

*   Avatar images
*   Banner images
*   Organization logos

### Metadata Storage[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#metadata-storage "Direct link to Metadata Storage")

Store structured metadata on IPFS:

*   Thing descriptions
*   Organization details
*   Person profiles

Quick Start[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#quick-start "Direct link to Quick Start")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    import { GraphQLClient } from 'graphql-request'import { API_URL_PROD } from '@0xintuition/graphql'const client = new GraphQLClient(API_URL_PROD)// Upload an image from URLconst imageResult = await client.request(`  mutation UploadImageFromUrl($url: String!) {    uploadImageFromUrl(url: $url) {      hash      url      size    }  }`, {  url: 'https://example.com/image.png'})// Upload JSON metadataconst jsonResult = await client.request(`  mutation UploadJsonToIpfs($json: JSON!) {    uploadJsonToIpfs(json: $json) {      hash      url    }  }`, {  json: {    name: 'My Atom',    description: 'Description here',    image: imageResult.uploadImageFromUrl.url  }})console.log('Metadata URL:', jsonResult.uploadJsonToIpfs.url)// Use this URL when creating the atom

IPFS URL Format[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#ipfs-url-format "Direct link to IPFS URL Format")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All uploaded content is accessible via IPFS:

    ipfs://<hash>

The API returns both the raw hash and a gateway URL:

    {  "hash": "QmXnnyufdzAWL5CqZ2RnSNgPbvCc1ALT73s6epPrRnZ1Xy",  "url": "ipfs://QmXnnyufdzAWL5CqZ2RnSNgPbvCc1ALT73s6epPrRnZ1Xy"}

File Size Limits[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#file-size-limits "Direct link to File Size Limits")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Upload Type | Maximum Size |
| --- | --- |
| Image (base64) | 5 MB |
| Image from URL | 10 MB |
| JSON | 1 MB |

Supported Image Formats[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#supported-image-formats "Direct link to Supported Image Formats")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   JPEG / JPG
*   PNG
*   GIF
*   WebP
*   SVG

Related Documentation[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/mutations/images/overview#related-documentation "Direct link to Related Documentation")

# Overview | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/overview#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Loading diagram...

Intuition provides GraphQL APIs for querying its knowledge graph in a convenient and versatile way.

**Available endpoints**

### 

Intuition mainnet

    https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql

[Try on GraphQL Explorer](https://studio.apollographql.com/sandbox/explorer?endpoint=https://mainnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql)

### 

Intuition testnet

    https://testnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql

[Try on GraphQL Explorer](https://studio.apollographql.com/sandbox/explorer?endpoint=https://testnet.intuition.sh/v1/graphql)

Alternatively, you can import these URLs from the GraphQL package:

    import { API_URL_PROD, API_URL_DEV } from '@0xintuition/graphql'

If this is your first time using GraphQL, you can learn more at [graphql.org](https://graphql.org/)
.

There are a few ways to get started with this GraphQL API, depending on the level of abstraction and customization you require :

[### \
\
SDK\
\
Integrate with Intuition smart contracts using our TypeScript SDK.](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-sdk/installation-and-setup)
[### \
\
Custom Queries\
\
Create bespoke GraphQL queries for your specific use case.](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/custom-queries)

### GraphQL Package[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/overview#graphql-package "Direct link to GraphQL Package")

[### \
\
Install it with NPM\
\
Install and use the NPM package with ready-made React Query hooks.](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/npm-package)
[### \
\
Use it as a GraphQL Generator\
\
Use the GraphQL package to generate your own queries and mutations.](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/npm-package)

See GraphQL in Action[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/overview#see-graphql-in-action "Direct link to See GraphQL in Action")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Check out these complete tutorials using the GraphQL API:

*   **[Finding Top Dapps](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/tutorials/queries/finding-top-dapps-on-coinbase)
    ** - Query and rank decentralized applications by market cap
*   **[Building Activity Feeds](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/tutorials/queries/building-user-activity-feeds)
    ** - Create real-time user activity streams
*   **[Discovering Trusted Accounts](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/tutorials/queries/discovering-most-trusted-accounts)
    ** - Build trust graphs and find reliable accounts
*   **[Finding Related Claims](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/tutorials/queries/finding-related-claims)
    ** - Traverse the knowledge graph to find connections

[More tutorials →](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/tutorials/overview)

Interactive Playground[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/overview#interactive-playground "Direct link to Interactive Playground")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Try the Intuition GraphQL API straight from your browser! The playground below connects to the live testnet endpoint and allows you to explore the knowledge graph in real time.

Query AtomsQuery TriplesQuery User PositionsSearch Atoms by LabelGet Vault StatisticsQuery AccountsQuery Atom by IDQuery Triple by IDQuery User Activity

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GetAtoms {
  atoms(limit: 10, order\_by: { created\_at: desc }) {
    term\_id
    label
    emoji
    type
    image
    created\_at
    block\_number
    creator {
      id
      label
    }
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

# Vault Events | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Functions for parsing vault-related events from transaction receipts.

eventParseDeposited[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#eventparsedeposited "Direct link to eventParseDeposited")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse Deposited events from a transaction.

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#returns "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    sender: Address    receiver: Address    vaultId: bigint    assets: bigint    shares: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#example "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseDeposited } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseDeposited(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('Vault ID:', event.args.vaultId)  console.log('Deposited:', formatEther(event.args.assets))  console.log('Shares received:', formatEther(event.args.shares))})

* * *

eventParseRedeemed[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#eventparseredeemed "Direct link to eventParseRedeemed")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse Redeemed events from a transaction.

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#returns-1 "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    sender: Address    receiver: Address    vaultId: bigint    assets: bigint    shares: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#example-1 "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseRedeemed } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseRedeemed(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('Vault ID:', event.args.vaultId)  console.log('Shares redeemed:', formatEther(event.args.shares))  console.log('Assets received:', formatEther(event.args.assets))})

* * *

eventParseSharePriceChanged[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#eventparsesharepricechanged "Direct link to eventParseSharePriceChanged")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Parse SharePriceChanged events.

### Returns[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#returns-2 "Direct link to Returns")

    Promise<Array<{  args: {    vaultId: bigint    oldPrice: bigint    newPrice: bigint  }}>>

### Example[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/events/vault-events#example-2 "Direct link to Example")

    import { eventParseSharePriceChanged } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const events = await eventParseSharePriceChanged(publicClient, txHash)events.forEach((event) => {  console.log('Vault ID:', event.args.vaultId)  console.log('Old price:', formatEther(event.args.oldPrice))  console.log('New price:', formatEther(event.args.newPrice))})

* * *

# Protocol Package Overview | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The `@0xintuition/protocol` package is a comprehensive TypeScript/JavaScript SDK for interacting with the Intuition onchain knowledge graph. It provides low-level access to build, query, and manage atoms (entities), triples (statements), vaults, and bonding rewards on the Intuition blockchain.

What is the Protocol Package?[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#what-is-the-protocol-package "Direct link to What is the Protocol Package?")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Protocol package is built on top of [viem](https://viem.sh/)
, providing type-safe, performant access to all Intuition smart contracts. It handles:

*   **Atom Management**: Create and query entities (people, organizations, concepts)
*   **Triple Management**: Create statements connecting atoms (subject-predicate-object)
*   **Vault Operations**: Deposit and redeem assets, manage shares
*   **Trust Bonding**: Participate in epoch-based rewards and staking
*   **Event Parsing**: Extract structured data from transaction events
*   **Configuration**: Query protocol parameters and fees

Installation[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#installation "Direct link to Installation")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install the package along with its peer dependency `viem`:

*   npm
*   pnpm
*   yarn
*   bun

    npm install viem @0xintuition/protocol

    pnpm install viem @0xintuition/protocol

    yarn add viem @0xintuition/protocol

    bun install viem @0xintuition/protocol

**Peer Dependencies:** `viem ^2.0.0`

Quick Start[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#quick-start "Direct link to Quick Start")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here's a minimal example to create an atom:

    import {  intuitionTestnet,  getMultiVaultAddressFromChainId,  multiVaultCreateAtoms,  multiVaultGetAtomCost,  eventParseAtomCreated,} from '@0xintuition/protocol'import { createPublicClient, createWalletClient, http, toHex } from 'viem'// Setup clientsconst publicClient = createPublicClient({  chain: intuitionTestnet,  transport: http(),})const walletClient = createWalletClient({  chain: intuitionTestnet,  transport: http(),  account, // your account (metamask, private key, etc)})// Get contract addressconst address = getMultiVaultAddressFromChainId(intuitionTestnet.id)// Create an atomconst atomCost = await multiVaultGetAtomCost({ address, publicClient })const atomUri = toHex('Hello, Intuition!')const txHash = await multiVaultCreateAtoms(  { address, walletClient, publicClient },  {    args: [[atomUri], [atomCost]],    value: atomCost,  })// Parse the created atomconst events = await eventParseAtomCreated(publicClient, txHash)console.log('Atom ID:', events[0].args.termId)

Key Features[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#key-features "Direct link to Key Features")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Type-Safe Interactions[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#type-safe-interactions "Direct link to Type-Safe Interactions")

Built on viem for full TypeScript support with auto-completion and type inference:

    import type { ReadConfig, WriteConfig } from '@0xintuition/protocol'// Read-only operationsconst readConfig: ReadConfig = {  address: contractAddress,  publicClient: publicClient,}// Write operations (transactions)const writeConfig: WriteConfig = {  address: contractAddress,  publicClient: publicClient,  walletClient: walletClient,}

### Multi-Network Support[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#multi-network-support "Direct link to Multi-Network Support")

Works seamlessly across Intuition Mainnet and Testnet:

    import { intuitionMainnet, intuitionTestnet } from '@0xintuition/protocol'// Mainnet (Chain ID: 1155)const mainnetClient = createPublicClient({  chain: intuitionMainnet,  transport: http(),})// Testnet (Chain ID: 13579)const testnetClient = createPublicClient({  chain: intuitionTestnet,  transport: http(),})

### Comprehensive Event Parsing[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#comprehensive-event-parsing "Direct link to Comprehensive Event Parsing")

Extract structured data from transaction receipts:

    import {  eventParseAtomCreated,  eventParseTripleCreated,  eventParseDeposited,} from '@0xintuition/protocol'// Parse multiple event types from a single transactionconst atomEvents = await eventParseAtomCreated(publicClient, txHash)const depositEvents = await eventParseDeposited(publicClient, txHash)console.log('Atom ID:', atomEvents[0]?.args.termId)console.log('Shares received:', depositEvents[0]?.args.shares)

### Batch Operations[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#batch-operations "Direct link to Batch Operations")

Optimize gas costs with batch operations:

    import { multiVaultDepositBatch } from '@0xintuition/protocol'const txHash = await multiVaultDepositBatch(  { address, walletClient, publicClient },  {    args: [      receiverAddress,      [vaultId1, vaultId2, vaultId3], // Multiple vaults      [curveId1, curveId2, curveId3], // Bonding curves      [assets1, assets2, assets3],     // Deposit amounts      [minShares1, minShares2, minShares3], // Slippage protection    ],    value: assets1 + assets2 + assets3,  })

Package Structure[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#package-structure "Direct link to Package Structure")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Protocol package is organized into several modules:

*   **MultiVault Operations**: Core atom, triple, and vault functions
*   **Trust Bonding**: Epoch-based rewards and staking
*   **Wrapped Trust**: Native token wrapping/unwrapping
*   **Event Parsing**: Transaction event extraction
*   **Configuration**: Protocol parameters and deployments
*   **Types**: TypeScript type definitions
*   **ABIs**: Contract ABIs for all protocol contracts

When to Use Protocol Package[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#when-to-use-protocol-package "Direct link to When to Use Protocol Package")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Protocol package is ideal when you need:

*   **Low-level control**: Direct access to contract functions with minimal abstraction
*   **Performance**: Optimized viem-based interactions
*   **Batch operations**: Create multiple atoms/triples or deposit to multiple vaults in one transaction
*   **Event parsing**: Extract detailed event data from transactions
*   **Custom workflows**: Build your own higher-level abstractions

For simpler, more user-friendly interactions, consider using the [SDK Package](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-sdk/quick-start)
 which provides higher-level abstractions including IPFS pinning, JSON-LD support, and simplified APIs.

Next Steps[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#next-steps "Direct link to Next Steps")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Configuration Guide](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/configuration)
     - Set up clients and networks
*   [API Reference](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/api-reference/multivault/atoms)
     - Explore all available functions
*   [Examples](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/examples/creating-atoms-triples)
     - See complete workflows

Resources[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#resources "Direct link to Resources")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Repository**: [github.com/0xIntuition/intuition-ts](https://github.com/0xIntuition/intuition-ts)

*   **Viem Documentation**: [viem.sh](https://viem.sh/)
    

See Also[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/protocol/getting-started/overview#see-also "Direct link to See Also")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [SDK Package](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/intuition-sdk/quick-start)
     - Higher-level API with IPFS and JSON-LD support

# Use Pre-Computed Statistics | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Leverage time-series aggregation tables for efficient analytics.

Anti-Pattern[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#anti-pattern "Direct link to Anti-Pattern")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # BAD: Computing trends from raw eventsquery GetPriceHistory($termId: String!) {  share_price_changes(    where: { term_id: { _eq: $termId } }    order_by: { updated_at: asc }  ) {    updated_at    share_price    total_assets    total_shares  }}# Then computing daily aggregates in application code

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    # GOOD: Using pre-computed daily statisticsquery GetDailyPriceStats($termId: String!, $curveId: numeric!) {  share_price_change_stats_daily(    where: {      term_id: { _eq: $termId }      curve_id: { _eq: $curveId }    }    order_by: { bucket: desc }    limit: 30  ) {    bucket    first_share_price    last_share_price    difference    change_count  }}

Available Tables[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#available-tables "Direct link to Available Tables")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `share_price_change_stats_hourly/daily/weekly/monthly`
*   `signal_stats_hourly/daily/monthly`

Benefits[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#benefits "Direct link to Benefits")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **Faster queries**: Pre-aggregated data
2.  **Less computation**: Done server-side
3.  **Smaller payloads**: Aggregated vs raw data
4.  **Better UX**: Faster dashboard loading

*   [Anti-Pattern](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#anti-pattern)
    
*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#best-practice)
    
*   [Available Tables](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#available-tables)
    
*   [Benefits](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/pre-computed-stats#benefits)

---

# Error Handling | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Handle GraphQL errors gracefully in your application.

Error Structure[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#error-structure "Direct link to Error Structure")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    {  "data": null,  "errors": [    {      "message": "Field 'nonexistent_field' not found in type 'atoms'",      "extensions": {        "path": "$.selectionSet.atoms.selectionSet.nonexistent_field",        "code": "validation-failed"      }    }  ]}

Error Types[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#error-types "Direct link to Error Types")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **validation-failed**: Query syntax or schema validation error
*   **constraint-violation**: Database constraint violated
*   **unexpected**: Internal server error

Best Practice[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#best-practice "Direct link to Best Practice")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    import { GraphQLClient, ClientError } from 'graphql-request'try {  const data = await client.request(query, variables)  console.log('Success:', data)} catch (error) {  if (error instanceof ClientError) {    console.error('GraphQL errors:', error.response.errors)    console.error('Status:', error.response.status)  } else {    console.error('Network error:', error)  }}

Tips[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#tips "Direct link to Tips")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **Check errors array**: GraphQL can return partial data with errors
2.  **Log error details**: Include query and variables for debugging
3.  **Show user-friendly messages**: Don't expose internal errors
4.  **Implement retry logic**: For network failures

*   [Error Structure](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#error-structure)
    
*   [Error Types](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#error-types)
    
*   [Best Practice](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#best-practice)
    
*   [Tips](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/best-practices/error-handling#tips)

---

# Trending Atoms | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/trending-atoms#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Find top atoms ranked by vault market cap.

Query[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/trending-atoms#query "Direct link to Query")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GetTrendingAtoms($curveId: numeric!, $limit: Int!) {
  vaults(
    where: { curve\_id: { \_eq: $curveId } }
    order\_by: { market\_cap: desc }
    limit: $limit
  ) {
    term\_id
    market\_cap
    position\_count
    total\_shares
    term {
      atom {
        term\_id
        label
        image
        type
      }
    }
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

*   [Query](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/trending-atoms#query)

# Price History | Intuition Docs

[Skip to main content](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/price-history#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Analyze share price trends using time-series data.

Query[​](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/price-history#query "Direct link to Query")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Query

▶ Run Query

query GetPriceTrends($termId: String!, $curveId: numeric!, $days: Int!) {
  share\_price\_change\_stats\_daily(
    where: {
      term\_id: { \_eq: $termId }
      curve\_id: { \_eq: $curveId }
    }
    order\_by: { bucket: desc }
    limit: $days
  ) {
    bucket
    first\_share\_price
    last\_share\_price
    difference
    change\_count
  }
}

#### Variables

Click "Run Query" to execute the GraphQL query and see results

*   [Query](https://www.docs.intuition.systems/docs/graphql-api/examples/price-history#query)
